sources.notes: Easy version: all operation costs are effectively one, so the answer is only 0, 1, or 2.
Official editorial checks common prime divisors in a_i for answer 0 and in a_i+1 for answer 1.

annotation.summary: Find the minimum number of +1 operations needed so that two array elements have gcd greater than 1.
annotation.constraints: Values are at most about 2e5, so prime divisors can be precomputed by sieve or factored directly in the easy version.
annotation.core_idea: The answer is never above 2 because two elements can each be made even with at most one increment. Therefore it is enough to test answer 0 by checking whether two numbers share a prime factor, and answer 1 by temporarily replacing one a_i with a_i+1 and checking whether it shares a prime factor with another number.
annotation.complexity: O(N log log N + n omega(N)) with precomputed prime divisors.
tricks: answer bounded by making two numbers even

solution_variants.name: prime-factor-presence
solution_variants.summary: Maintain counts of prime divisors of current numbers, then test shared factors for a_i and a_i+1.
solution_variants.complexity: O(N log log N + n omega(N))

tags.evidence: The solution only needs the set/count of prime divisors appearing among array values.
The target condition is gcd(a_i, a_j)>1, equivalently sharing a prime divisor.
The global optimum is reduced greedily to checking 0, then 1, otherwise 2.
